data.title: XRPT Can Amplify The Daily Performance Of XRP
data.description:
Volatility Shares' 2x XRP ETF (NASDAQ:XRPT) is a leveraged strategy designed to provide traders with 2x the daily performance of Ripple (XRP-USD). Because this is a leveraged strategy, XRPT should only be used for daily exposure and should not be considered as a long-term investment vehicle.

XRP Background

XRP is one of the most heavily traded cryptocurrencies, with recent volumes being in the $11b per day range. XRP Ledger was launched in 2012 as an open-source, permissionless, and decentralized technology designed to enhance transaction speed. XRP has historically received substantial support across financial institutions, aiming to enhance efficiency, reduce transaction time, and offer an alternative for international payment services.

XRP recently had a major win against a 2020 legal case brought by the SEC. On August 7, 2025, the lawsuit was abandoned with a $125mm fine to the SEC.

XRPT Features

XRPT is a leveraged strategy designed to provide 2x the daily performance of XRP. This strategy executes this by investing in XRP futures, providing investors with 200% exposure.

Futures are settled on a daily basis, meaning that profits and losses determine margin requirements at the end of each trading. If losses mount, the counterparty may request additional collateral in good faith of covering at settlement. As a result of daily settlement, performance will reset on a daily basis, making performance metrics meaningful on a daily basis.

This is reflected in XRPT as an investment vehicle in which performance is reset on a daily basis. This means that returns can be compounded over time, resulting in returns above or below the 2x daily performance target. For this reason, XRPT should only be held for no longer than a single trading period. Holding periods longer than a single trading day can also result in value decay.

Comparing the 1-month performance between XRPT and XRP, XRP substantially outperformed XRPT in terms of total returns. Because of the daily reset, returns over long periods of time will not reflect the indicated target return profile. This means that during periods of significant price increases or decreases, XRPT can substantially deviate in terms of performance, resulting in differentiated returns.

Comparing daily performance, XRPT may not achieve its target 2x daily performance depending on trading volumes and volatility. There may be other underlying factors at play relating to the pricing of the futures contracts that may also impact the pricing of the ETF.

Nonetheless, there may be periods where XRPT returns exceed those of XRP.

Looking at liquidity, XRPT is a relatively small portfolio strategy with $125mm in assets under management, with an average of 573k shares changing hands on a daily basis. Despite the strong liquidity, XRPT exhibits some spread risk, or cost-in/cost-out, with a 24 bps spread. The strategy has a relatively low expense ratio of 94 bps.

By comparison, peer strategy Teucrium 2x Long Daily XRP ETF (XXRP) has a relatively larger fee of 189 bps. Despite the higher expense ratio, XXRP may provide traders with significantly more depth with $462mm in AUM and an average trading volume of 1.11mm shares.

Risks Related to Leveraged Strategies

There are inherent risks in investing in leveraged strategies. Because these strategies are meant to achieve their stated multiple over the underlying index on a daily basis, holding these strategies beyond a single day can lead to skewed performance, especially following consecutive days of upside or downside risk. This can lead to a compounding effect as well as value decay as a result of the daily reset in the derivatives positions.

Leveraged strategies also add an extra layer of risk to amplified downside potential. For example, if the underlying index were to appreciate by 2% in a single day, a 3x strategy would appreciate by 6%, meaning that an investment of $100 would increase to $106. If the following day results in a -2% decline in the underlying index or a -6% decline in the leveraged strategy, the investment will decline to $99.64.

Holding leveraged strategies for longer than a single day can lead to a compounding effect on one’s position, potentially leading to significant skews from the intended multiplier. During multiple days of downside risk, an investor can experience significant value decay, potentially resulting in the inability to recover losses.

Liquidity must be considered prior to investing in a leveraged fund strategy. Given that these funds are designed to be traded daily, trading volumes must be considered to ensure that cost-in/cost-out isn’t deteriorated by spreads.

Traders must also consider additional taxes on short-term capital gains as well as trading fees prior to considering a leveraged trading strategy.

You can review additional risks here and here.

Before making a trading or investment decision in leveraged strategies, be sure to fully understand your risk profile and set a trading mandate to manage these risks. I believe preemptively setting parameters for selling out of a position is prudent in ensuring that an investor appropriately manages downside risk.

Final Thoughts

XRPT can be an appealing strategy for traders seeking amplified exposure to the daily performance of XRP. Because XRPT is a leveraged strategy, this ETF should only be used for daily exposure.

data.title: Retail Sales Up 0.5% In July, Slightly Lower Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Census Bureau's Advance Retail Sales Report for July showed consumer spending was slightly lower than expected, with headline sales rising 0.5%. This marks the second consecutive monthly increase and was just below the expected 0.6% rise.

For an inflation-adjusted perspective on retail sales, take a look at our Real Retail Sales commentary.

Here is the introduction from today's report:

Advance estimates of U.S. retail and food services sales for July 2025, adjusted for seasonal variation and holiday and trading-day differences, but not for price changes, were $726.3 billion, up 0.5 percent (±0.4 percent) from the previous month, and up 3.9 percent (±0.5 percent) from July 2024. Total sales for the May 2025 through July 2025 period were up 3.9 percent (±0.4 percent) from the same period a year ago. The May 2025 to June 2025 percent change was revised from up 0.6 percent (±0.5 percent) to up 0.9 percent (±0.2 percent).Retail trade sales were up 0.7 percent (±0.5 percent) from June 2025, and up 3.7 percent (±0.5 percent) from last year. Nonstore retailers were up 8.0 percent (±1.2 percent) from last year, while food service and drinking places were up 5.6 percent (±1.8 percent) from July 2024.

The chart below is a log-scale snapshot of retail sales since the early 1990s. The three exponential regressions through the data help us to evaluate the long-term trend of this key economic indicator.

  1. The light purple line is a linear regression through the complete data series.
  2. The green line is a regression from the start of the series through the end of 2007 and then extrapolated to the present - thus excluding the Financial Crisis.
  3. The blue line is a regression from the start of the series through the end of 2019 and then extrapolated to the present - thus excluding the COVID-19 pandemic.

Monthly retail sales have been above the light purple and blue line since March 2021, signaling increased consumer spending that was most likely pent-up as a result of the pandemic.

The year-over-year percent change provides another perspective on the historical trend. Current retail sales are up 3.9% compared to one year ago. Here is the headline series with a callout to the most recent 12 months.

Core Retail Sales

Core sales (ex Autos) were up 0.3% in July. This is down from June's 0.8% reading and consistent with the forecast.

Core retail sales are up 3.7% compared to one year ago. Here is the year-over-year chart of core retail sales with a callout to the most recent 12 months.

Retail Sales: “Control” Purchases

The next two charts illustrate retail sales “control” purchases, which is an even more “core” view of retail sales. This series excludes motor vehicles & parts, gasoline, building materials as well as food services & drinking places. The popular financial press typically ignores this series, but it's a more consistent and reliable reading of the economy. Retail sales control purchases rose 0.5% in July. This is down from June's 0.8% reading and better than the expected 0.4% growth in control sales.

Similar to the retail sales snapshot chart earlier, the chart below is a log-scale snapshot of control purchases since the early 1990s and includes two of the exponential regressions previously mentioned.

Here is the same series year-over-year. Current control purchases are up 4.9% compared to one year ago.

For a better sense of the reduced volatility of the “control” series, here is a YoY overlay with the headline retail sales. Note that the two series follow each other closely, but headline sales have more extreme highs and lows than the control series.

Retail sales will impact interest in the SPDR S&P Retail ETF (XRT), VanEck Retail ETF (RTH), Amplify Online Retail ETF (IBUY), and ProShares Online Retail ETF (ONLN).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Village Farms Stock Should Be Sold
data.description:
The cannabis sector has exploded higher this week, with most of the stocks flying higher, including the "bad" ones. I do have still have Strong Sells on some stocks that I think are bad, and I have reduced to Neutral some good ones, like Village Farms International, Inc. (NASDAQ:VFF). It is in the index I aim to beat (and am beating), and I like the company, but the price is too high, and I am reducing it to Sell, which I explain in this article.

I Like Village Farms (the Company)

I started liking Village Farms a little in early 2023, and I put in a Strong Buy rating in January of 2023 after it plunged following an equity offering, explaining why it was deep value. The stock was then $0.99, and it stayed cheap for a long time. I stayed with the Strong Buy Rating for a while, writing two more articles about it here at Seeking Alpha. I downgraded it right before the cannabis market peaked in late April of 2024 at a price of $1.37 to Neutral, explaining why I no longer included it in my model portfolio at 420 Investor. In July of 2024, I upgraded it again to Strong Buy at $0.94, explaining why I liked Village Farms so much. I downgraded it to Hold very recently in late July at a price of $1.52, saying why it was time to be on the sidelines.

I still like the company, but I am downgrading it to Sell today. I kept my Strong Buy in May after the stock had run up a lot explaining why the move the company made by privatizing their produce business was a game changer. I did mention a few risks, but I discussed how the stock was not widely followed on Seeking Alpha, how VFF was still cheap despite the rally to $1.09, and how it faced such opportunities internationally. It was a bear market still for cannabis stocks, but I stuck with this stock, which was on a big rally in contrast to its peers.

Village Farms is an excellent Canadian LP. They have made some mistakes in my view (the CBD acquisition), but they have done very well in Canada with Pure Sunfarms and in other countries. I have said before and still believe that their CEO, Michael DeGiglio, is strong, and I have called out their COO, Ann Lefever, who I have spoken with. Insiders own a lot of stock, too, including their CFO, and they added after the spin-out was announced.

VFF Has Soared

Village Farms is now up 265.8% year-to-date, posting a three year high recently. From the low of $0.45 in early April, it has soared now 527% to $2.82:

There are a couple of gaps in trading on this six-month chart, including the move in May, when they announced their Q1 financials and the then-pending spin-out, as well as this week. I have said before and still believe that the gap from $0.71 to $0.80 will not need to be filled, but I am expecting the gap from Monday that goes down to $1.72 will. My current first support of $1.90 is above that level and below the open from Monday of $1.93.

Comparing it to the other Canadian LPs that are in the New Cannabis Ventures Global Cannabis Stock Index, the stock, which joined the index on 6/30, is up a lot more than its peers in 2025:

Taking a longer-term perspective from late 2022, VFF has outperformed strongly:

Going up a lot is not bad in and of itself, but the stock has left some gaps along the way and has had very high volume this week, a multiple of its very high volume when they announced the spinoff of the produce business in May.

Bad Buyer of VFF

I follow AdvisorShares Pure US Cannabis ETF (MSOS) very closely, and they now have a position in VFF of 1.36 million shares. As of August 1, they held just 250K shares, so they have been buying this month. It is still a small position at just 0.5% of the portfolio, its 13th in size out of 19 names. The ETF still holds massive positions in its top 3 names that total 69%.

VFF is not an MSO, though it may become one. It has an asset in Texas, a greenhouse, and it could get one of the 12 licenses that the state is handing out, as I discussed in that last article. VFF has had a lot of buyers apparently, but this is the only one that I can demonstrate. MSOS is not a trader, but it can't overcome the force that I discuss below.

A Future Seller

As I have pointed out in many articles since that one from 1/30/23, Village Farms issued 18.35 million warrants at $1.65 in that financing, and they are now deeply in-the-money. There has been no updated since the filings this month about any exercises, though some may have been exercised.

The good news for the holders of those warrants is that they do not have to do anything right now, as the expiration of them is not until 2028. The good news for the company is that it will increase the cash balance by $30 million.

VFF Is Expensive Now

Besides the potential pressure of profit-taking or warrant exercises, the stock is not likely to have more buyers joining it. When I called it a deep value stock, it was trading at just 0.43X tangible book value, and it stayed cheap a very long time. Assuming the warrants and also 2.93 million options at $0.76-.82 are exercised, there are 136.6 million shares outstanding, and the tangible book value, reported at $213.8 million at the end of Q2, rises to $248.5 million. The market cap of $385 million is 1.56X this adjusted level. That is not a reason to sell, but the downside risk has increased. I follow 5 Canadian LPs closely, and they range from 1.0X to 1.8X now, with VFF the second highest.

I think that investors care about valuation relative to earnings, and in cannabis most analysts use enterprise value to projected adjusted EBITDA. For year-end targets, I am focused on the 2026 estimates. According to Koyfin, the outlook for 2026 for Village Farms by the analysts is adjusted EBITDA of $45.7 million. By this estimate, the stock trades at just 7.5X, but I think that the estimate may be too high.

In Q2, the company reported adjusted EBITDA of $17.1 million, up from $2.9 million in 2024-Q2, but it had a one-time factor helping it, as they explained in the press release:

Adjusted EBITDA from continuing produce operations improved to $6.4 million from $0.1 million for the quarter ended June 30, 2024 due to both a vendor settlement and improved continuing produce operations.

The company gave no guidance in its press release or on the conference call. The current revenue outlook according to Koyfin for 2026 is $247.2 million, and $45.7 million would be a margin of 18.5%. I think this is way too high!

Ahead of the Q2 report, the outlook was for adjusted EBITDA in 2026 of $31.7 million on revenue of $215.6 million, a margin of 14.7%, a margin that I thought might be too high. Using 15% instead of the current 18.5% would yield $37.1 million. raising the enterprise value to projected adjusted EBITDA for 2026 to 9.2X.

It is a federally legal company, and it may continue to do very well internationally, though things can go backwards there too. It remains to be seen if it will be able to operate in the U.S. and keep its NASDAQ listing. Two Canadian LPs do have MSO operations in the U.S. that are carried on the balance sheets and that do not run through their income statements. I am actually thinking that if VFF gets a win in Texas medical cannabis, it could do very well there. Even if rescheduling does go through, though, it's not clear that they will be able to run the program through its income statement on its financials. Unlike its peers, VFF has no Canadian listing.

The earnings might not be as high as the estimate suggests them to be, and the company has net cash. My year-end target is now $3.03, slightly higher than the current price but way below the level it could reach if the prospects fade and it gets down to tangible book value of $1.80 per share (assuming the warrants don't get exercised).

Conclusion

I downgraded VRR to Neutral too early, but I am cutting it to Sell now for the reasons I have explained, mainly the warrant situation but also the outperformance relative to other cannabis stocks. The stock has nearly doubled since that downgrade in late July! The whole Canadian LP sector has soared, and I own only 1 Canadian LP in my model portfolio. Village Farms is in the index that I manage the model portfolio against with a current weighting of 8.0%, and I hold none. The 7 Canadian LPs that are in that index total currently 31.8%, and my exposure to that one LP that I include is 14.3%.

Canada could get a lift, as I have said in the past, if the excise tax is changed from a fixed-cost per unit cultivated to a floating rate that is lower, but there are no signs yet of this being under active discussion.

I have zero exposure to the MSOs, which have 3 names that qualified and that represent 15.5%. While I have 20% cash, the maximum permitted by my rules, I do have 65.6% exposure to four ancillary companies. Ancillaries are currently 31.8% of the Global Cannabis Stock Index. I think that some of the ancillaries are a better way to invest in cannabis right now.

So, Village Farms is a good company and not a good stock right now! I hope to buy it back near that warrant exercise price of $1.65. For those who want to stay with it, one good thing could happen: A strategic buyer could become interested. This has not worked well for the alcohol companies and tobacco companies that have invested in Canadian LPs, but it could happen. I would question, though, why they waited so long!

data.title: TeraWulf: Google-Backed HPC Contract Drives Momentum (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
TeraWulf Overview

TeraWulf Inc. (NASDAQ:WULF) might well be considered a late mover among the Bitcoin (BTC-USD) miners in high performance computing [HPC]. They only began any serious HPC pivot last year when they announced (in their September 2024 monthly production update) the successful completion of their 2MW proof of concept project called “Wolf Den” to showcase their HPC hosting capabilities. Despite the late pivot, TeraWulf is now targeting the right opportunities and building a strong HPC backlog. The latest $3.7 billion contract with Google (GOOG)-backed Fluidstack is a prime example of well-timed execution resulting in successful contracts.

There are several things TearWulf has done right to get here. One of them being that their HPC pivot strategy has been less exposed to GPU hardware lifecycles and depreciation because CaPex allocated to HPC has been focused more on power and infrastructure, which retain value longer than GPUs. TeraWulf does not maintain a large, depreciating fleet of GPUs for revenue generation. Spendings on GPU for the company’s Compute business line recorded so far was when the proof-of-concept Wolf Den was being built, which involved operating a compact Nvidia A100 GPU system at the Lake Mariner Facility. And this strategic execution was the highlight for me when I last covered WULF in May. Looking back, I think the more cautious Hold rating was overly conservative, and a Buy would have been a better call.

I believe TeraWulf is now at the "right place at the right time" in HPC hosting. Among the miners, TeraWulf can be considered a late bird in HPC hosting. I believe this has allowed TeraWulf to craft a better strategy for the WULF Compute business line.

Sometimes it’s the ones who come late to the party that leave with the best gains, after watching the early guests spill their drinks. In its HPC approach, TeraWulf is avoiding the operational complexity that purchasing and maintaining GPUs for HPC hosting presents, and will be focusing on providing infrastructure to HPC clients – power, cooling, rack, and space – as the management has iterated. TeraWulf’s HPC hosting is basically: bring your GPUs and we’ll host them. - Excerpt from my WULF coverage in May.

Bitcoin miners who pivoted to HPC earlier took the GPU risk and will likely incur the depreciation. I think it is risky enough for a Bitcoin miner to be exposed to crypto price cycles, then to compound that with further exposure to hardware cycles doubles the risk.

WULF is up 180% since visiting it on the release of the Q1 earnings in May, and is up ~59% since the Fluidstack-Google announcement yesterday. For investors who may have been on the sidelines, I believe the main question now is whether conditions support for more upside (from a fundamentals perspective), or if entry at this stage would be a case of an overhang of missed buying opportunity that presents the risk of catching the top, since WULF is already up a lot.

WULF Still Presents a Compelling Buy Despite Its Price Surge

Without beating around the bush, I strongly believe that the outlook for TeraWulf has changed greatly for the better, and I am upgrading my stance to Buy at this point. The pivot to HPC hosting has moved from talk to signed contracts, and revenue recognition from HPC is already underway, beginning Q3 2025, according to management’s guidance in the Q2 earnings call.

The outlook for strong HPC revenue is now highly visible. The Fluidstack agreements anchor about $3.7 billion of contracted revenue over ten year period, with two five-year options that lift the total to about $8.7 billion, under a modified gross lease (meaning TeraWulf is paid a fixed fee for infrastructure services while most variable costs are passed through to the client, keeping revenue predictable and limiting operational risk).

Google will backstop $1.8 billion of those obligations and receive warrants equal to roughly 8 percent pro forma WULF ownership. The deployment will start with about 40 MW online in the first half of 2026 (just months away) and the full 200+ MW by year end 2026.

Unit economics also look attractive and visible per management’s guidance. Management disclosed an expected site net operating income [NOI] margin of 85% on the Fluidstack deployment, which implies about $315 million of annual site NOI at full run rate ($3.7B contract / 10 years = $370M/yr in revenue; $370M x 0.85 NOI margin = ~$315M annual NOI). The revenue from the Fluidstack contract is highly predictable and grows over time due to built-in price escalations (starting around $370 million per year and rising over the 10-year contract). Under the modified gross lease, TeraWulf collects a fixed fee for providing power, cooling, and rack space, while most variable costs are passed through to Fluidstack, keeping revenue predictable and margins protected - an example of TeraWulf’s asset-light HPC hosting approach, which caught my attention in May.

The earlier Core42 lease also validates the above approach. Terms of the Core42 deal call for 60 MW of critical IT load at $125 per kilowatt per month, equal to $1.5 million per MW per year, with a 3% annual escalator and a twelve-month prepayment. Management framed EBITDA margin at ~70% and an unlevered return of 17 to 18% on a $6 million per MW build, which is compelling in today’s data center market. Scale optionality remains, with an expansion option up to 108 MW of additional critical load.

On the infrastructure side, TeraWulf’s power and sites provide strong capacity optionality for lessees. Lake Mariner has interconnection approval for 500 MW with applications pending to reach up to 750 MW, and the Cayuga site adds an 80-year ground lease with rights to develop up to 400 MW, with 138 MW expected ready online in 2026. These are zero-carbon, low-cost power markets with existing transmission and water, which shortens timelines. This infrastructure and power base is TeraWulf’s potential moat in AI infrastructure.

I believe TeraWulf’s financial base is on the verge of improving, while HPC ramps. Q2 2025 revenue was $47.6 million, up 34% YoY. Bitcoin mining capacity reached 12.8 EH/s also up around 45% YoY. Though net loss widened, TeraWulf returned to positive adjusted EBITDA of $14.5 million in Q2 - a notable improvement over Q1's negative adjusted EBITDA of -$4.7 million. Management guided to recognizing HPC hosting revenue beginning in Q3 2025, which marks an inflection point for TeraWulf's financial profile.

And at $3.88 billion EV and TTM revenue of $144.1 million, WULF currently trades around 27x EV/Sales. Now, with the $3.7 billion Fluidstack contract potentially adding roughly $370 million a year in revenue at full deployment, sales could potentially rise to over $500 million annually (if TeraWulf maintains around the current annual run rate from its current revenue streams).

If the market continues to value WULF at the same EV/Sales multiple of 27x, the enterprise value could increase proportionally to about $13.83 billion (or more conservatively above $10 billion). Assuming net debt remains stable (which is also plausible given early cash inflows from Fluidstack, projected high NOI margins, and phased capital spending as the rollout of HPC infrastructure will be in phases), this would imply a share price around 2.5x above current price (if we stick to our more conservative ~$10 billion EV). A ~2.5x or ~250% upside from the current $8.85 share price would make WULF trade around $22. Any expansion of the multiple due to factors like higher margins, as execution of the Fluidstack and Core42 contracts scale, would potentially push the upside further.

Risks and Takeaway

Execution risk remains real in the HPC hosting business, so I model in some cushion (a conservative 10–15% adjustment to projected revenue and phased deployment assumptions). The critical variables here are managing the construction schedule and coordinating with clients on the on-time arrival of their hardware. The Google backstop reduces counterparty risk on the Fluidstack deal. The prepayment and escalators on Core42 improve returns while the team funds and builds. I believe WULF's risk-reward skews positive at this point of the build phase.

TeraWulf's entry into HPC is based on an approach that minimizes capital and market risks. This approach makes WULF a Bitcoin miner worth being on the watch list. In a sector where timing and strategy are everything, TeraWulf may have just found its sweet spot in HPC. - Except for my WULF coverage in May.

In May, I initiated coverage with a takeaway that WULF was watchlist-worthy because of its HPC business approach. My current takeaway is simple: TeraWulf now has contracts, credible partners, clear unit economics, and secured power (Lake Mariner and Haynesville sites, totaling ~40 MW to start with full 200+ MW capacity by end of 2026). If you were sidelined earlier, I believe the setup has improved. I see enough line of sight to future cash flows to upgrade to Buy.

data.title: Hold On Vistra Corp. After Q2 2025 Earnings As Market Prices In Perfect Execution
data.description:
Vistra Corp. (NYSE:VST) is doing a lot right. The company is adding to its generation portfolio with a planned $1.9 billion Lotus acquisition, it just secured 20 more years of operating life for the Perry nuclear plant and it maintained its earnings outlook even after some operational setbacks. Management sees more than $6.8 billion in EBITDA by 2026, which gives a clear view of strong potential ahead.

The problem is that the market already seems to be pricing all of this in. The stock is up about 155 percent over the past year and trades at roughly 31 times forward earnings, which is much higher than what most utilities command. At this price, investors are assuming everything will go according to plan. Any delay in projects, unexpected outages, or weaker power prices could change that quickly. I see Vistra as a company worth holding, not rushing to buy, because the upside from here looks limited compared with the risks.

What the Company Does

Vistra runs both a retail and power generation business, with about 41 gigawatts of capacity in natural gas, nuclear, coal, solar and battery storage. On the retail side, it serves around five million customers across different states. That steady retail income helps balance the more volatile wholesale power business.

In recent years, it has moved to diversify further. The 2024 Energy Harbor merger brought in more nuclear capacity and retail customers. The planned Lotus acquisition will add 2,600 megawatts of natural gas generation in multiple U.S. regions. These moves make the company less dependent on any single market or fuel type.

Not everything has gone smoothly. A fire at the Moss Landing battery site permanently shut down 300 megawatts of storage and took another 450 megawatts offline for repairs. There was also a fire at the Martin Lake coal plant that will keep one unit out of service until late 2025. Insurance is covering much of the cost but these incidents highlight the challenges of running such a large and varied fleet.

Market Setting

The U.S. power market is changing fast. Demand is rising because of new data centers, electric vehicles and factories moving back to the country. At the same time, there is pressure to cut carbon emissions, which affects coal and gas plants.

Vistra operates in competitive power markets like ERCOT, PJM, ISO-NE, CAISO and NYISO. This gives it the ability to benefit when prices spike. Its nuclear and renewable assets could also qualify for federal or state incentives, which would help offset costs.

Competition is strong. Vistra goes up against some well-known names in the industry, including Constellation Energy (CEG) and NRG Energy (NRG).

Recent Financial Performance

In the second quarter of 2025, Vistra brought in $4.25 billion in revenue, up from $3.85 billion in the same period last year. That top-line growth did not fully carry through to profits. Operating income slipped from $808 million to $515 million, largely because fuel and delivery costs rose by a significant margin and the company recorded a $68 million asset write-down.

Margins remain strong for a utility. The EBIT margin is close to 20 percent, the gross margin sits around 40 percent, and the return on equity is nearly 39 percent. Those figures stand out in the sector, although they are helped by the company’s heavy use of leverage.

Debt sits at roughly $18.1 billion, putting the debt-to-equity ratio at more than 3.70. While cash flow is healthy, such high borrowing means the company is more vulnerable if earnings soften or interest costs climb.

Cash generated from operations in the first half of 2025 came in at $1.17 billion, which is down from $1.51 billion a year earlier. Working capital changes and costs from plant outages were the main reasons for the decline. Capital spending over the same period reached $1.46 billion, reflecting the scale of maintenance work and ongoing growth projects.

Valuation Check

Vistra’s own target for 2026 calls for about $6.8 billion in EBITDA. If you put a 10-times multiple on that, the business would be worth roughly $68 billion on an enterprise basis. Taking away the $18.08 billion in debt leaves close to $50 billion for shareholders. Spread across the roughly 339 million shares outstanding, that works out to about $148 a share.

Increasing the multiple to 11 times raises the enterprise value to around $74.8 billion. After subtracting the same debt, you end up with about $56.7 billion in equity, or roughly $167 per share. Even using this more generous assumption, the figure is still well below the current stock price of $202.5. That means the market is building in expectations that everything will run smoothly, from project execution to power pricing.

To me, this leaves very little cushion if the company hits a snag. Whether it is a delay in a major build, unexpected downtime at a plant, or softer demand, the valuation could give back some of that premium quickly.

Recent Headlines

The recent news cycle has leaned in Vistra’s favor. Second-quarter results beat expectations, the Lotus acquisition is on track and the extension of the nuclear license adds clarity to the long-term asset base. These developments have helped push the share price to an all-time high in early August 2025.

However, this upbeat tone also creates a risk. When expectations are already high, even a small setback can trigger a sharp reaction in the market.

Main Risks

Vistra operates a large and complex fleet of assets, which means unexpected outages are a constant possibility, as recent fires have shown. The company also has to integrate major acquisitions like Energy Harbor and Lotus and there is always the chance that combining operations will take longer or cost more than planned. High debt levels amplify the effect of any earnings drop. Exposure to swings in natural gas and power prices remains a factor, despite hedging strategies. On top of that, changes in environmental regulations could hurt the economics of coal and gas plants.

Looking Ahead

In the next year and a half, Vistra’s growth should be driven by rising electricity demand, stable nuclear generation and new capacity being added to the grid. Over the longer term, the Lotus plants and additional renewable projects should bring more stability to earnings and reduce reliance on any single fuel source.

Management expects 2025 EBITDA to land between $5.5 billion and $6.1 billion and is targeting more than $6.8 billion in 2026. With the stock already trading near record highs, there is very little room for results to come in below those targets without affecting the share price.

Possible Paths for the Stock

If commodity prices drop, integration takes longer, or outages occur again, the stock could slide to between $150 and $160. If the company simply meets current guidance, integrates Lotus on schedule and keeps operations stable, the price might stay in the $190 to $210 range. In a best-case scenario, where EBITDA tops $7 billion in 2026 and market conditions remain favorable, the stock could reach $230 to $240.

Final Take

Vistra is a strong, well-run company with a clear plan for growth. However, with the shares trading far above sector norms on earnings and cash flow, much of that growth story is already in the price. My view is that it makes sense to hold the stock at current levels. A drop below $170 would offer a much more attractive entry point. For now, this is a case of waiting rather than chasing, as the story is solid but the margin for upside is thin.

data.title: Arm Holdings' Meltdown Is Warranted - Growth Pains Ahead
data.description:
ARM's Mixed Growth Prospects Trigger Its Uncertain Investment Thesis - Swing Trade Potential Emerges

We previously covered Arm Holdings plc (NASDAQ:ARM) in July 2025, discussing how the rally had been well supported by the durable AI-related spending trends, the growing hyperscaler demand for custom silicon, and Nvidia's (NVDA) launch of Arm-based Grace Blackwell superchip.

Combined with the promising FQ1'26 guidance, the growing monetization/ royalty opportunities, and the growing server market share against the incumbent x86 platform, we had reiterated our Buy rating upon a deep correction to the secondary support levels of $120s ranges or the base support levels of $110s ranges for an improved margin of safety.

ARM 1Y Stock Price

Since then, our Buy zones have yet to materialize, as the ARM stock appears to be well supported at the $130s ranges despite the steep post-earnings correction by -16.9% at its worst.

For one, the company reports a top-line miss in the FQ1'26 earnings call while offering an underwhelming bottom-line guidance for FQ2'26, with it naturally spooking the market and investors as observed in the stock price underperformance afterwards.

Particularly, ARM has hinted at its new growth initiatives "beyond our current platform into additional compute to subsystems, chiplets and potentially full-end solutions."

We reckon that the ongoing transition is likely to be rather expensive indeed, attributed to the intensified R&D investments, the increased headcounts, and the higher Stock-Based Compensations, with the adj operating margins and adj EPS growth potentially underperforming historical trends/ market expectations.

These have yet to include any expensive M&A activities as well, while potentially being the fastest route to commercialization yet likely to trigger higher debt leveraging during an expensive borrowing cost environment and/ or dilutive new share issuances.

It remains to be seen how ARM may perform in these new arenas as well, attributed to the decades worth of headways boasted by its direct legacy peers, as Nvidia (NVDA) takes the throne for GPU/ AI accelerator leadership and Intel (INTC) remaining the king of the x86 jungle, no matter the market share losses to the dark horse, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD).

At the same time, hyperscalers are already transitioning to custom AI accelerators, as Broadcom (AVGO) previously highlighted its massive multi-year opportunities through ASICs, with an estimated FY2027 AI Serviceable Addressable Market [SAM] size of between $60B and $90B - with it defining "a potential target market size a business can reach."

These imply the massive competition ARM will face in the upcoming frontiers, with their efforts likely being a multi-year endeavor while underscoring its slower monetization cadence compared to its direct competitors.

While the future potential may be immense, since it may open up a new avenue for the company's future growth, assuming that it follows the typical fabless chipmaker route to reduce capex costs, we believe that it may be more prudent to wait and see how things develop in the mean time.

ARM's Mixed Performance Metrics

For now, given that ARM's FQ1'26 adj EPS performance at $0.35 (-36.3% QoQ/ -12.5% YoY) has underperformed FY2025 numbers at $1.63 (+28.3% YoY) and the 3Y historical growth profile at +35.8%, we can understand why the market has sold first and ask questions later.

Part of the culprit is naturally the drastically higher R&D efforts by +19% QoQ/ +34% YoY, with it well negating the richer gross margins of 97.9% (-0.5 points QoQ/ +0.5 YoY).

The over-reaction can be understood indeed, as discussed above, despite the robust adoption of ARM's core offerings, as observed in the higher Annualized Contract Value by +28% YoY, the growing total licenses by +36.3% YoY, the increased flexible licenses by +29.8% YoY, which naturally contributed to the expanding royalty revenue of $585M (-3.6% QoQ/ +25.2% YoY).

With the next few years likely to bring forth impacted bottom-line growth profile, we believe that the market will be closely monitoring its ongoing diversification efforts, with any miss against the consensus estimates likely to be punitive before monetization and renewed growth materializes.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

Combined with the management's underwhelming FQ2'26 adj EPS guidance of $0.33 at the midpoint (-5.7% QoQ/ +10% YoY), we can understand why the consensus have chosen to downgrade their forward estimates, with ARM expected to report a decelerating top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +19.9%/ +19.3% through FY2028.

This is compared to the last estimates of +20.7%/ +21.8% and the 3Y historical growth profile at +14%/ +35.8%, respectively, with it naturally underscoring the company's maturing growth profile despite the supposedly accretive impact from the ongoing AI boom.

ARM Valuations

These reasons are also why we believe that ARM does not appear compelling here, attributed to the relatively expensive FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 83.45x and FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 3.38x, compared to the sector median of 22.50x/ 1.79x, and the 2Y mean of 77.19x/ 1.29x, respectively.

Even if we compare against its computing platform/ semiconductor peers, including:

  • INTC at a 5Y average 12.43x/ 0.82x (prior to the PC demand destruction in 2022),
  • AMD currently at 47.11x/ 1.53x, and
  • NVDA currently at 42.10x/ 1.41x, respectively,

it goes without saying that there remains a minimal margin of safety at current inflated levels, with ARM's capital appreciation opportunity likely to underwhelm.

So, Is ARM Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

ARM 2Y Stock Price

For now, ARM has already lost part of its recent recovery albeit with the bulls defending the support level of $130s ranges, with it apparent that the stock is likely to continue trading sideways moving forward.

This is because there remains a great gap to our base-case fair-value estimates of $76.60, based on the LTM adj EPS of $1.59 (+12.7% sequentially) and the recent April 2025 P/E bottom of 48.21x (nearer to the peer group mean of 42x).

Thanks to the lowered consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates from $2.95 to $2.77, there remains a minimal margin of safety to our base-case long-term price target of $133.50 as well.

ARM's Nearly Oversold Position

Based on the technical indicators above, it seems that ARM may bottom soon, with it supporting our conjecture about its mixed return profile from current levels.

This is especially since we have observed a notable increase in its short interest volumes by +16.8% on a YoY basis, with any of its future gains likely to be moderated by aggressive short sellers.

On the other hand, these developments imply that ARM may be a suitable swing trade stock in the near-term, based on the approximate entry points at the established floor at the April 2024/ August 2024/ April 2025 bottom of $100s ranges and the approximate exit points at the intermediate resistance levels of $150s ranges.

Given its three monthly trade cycle (or potentially longer), it goes without saying that the stock is only suitable for those with a longer investing swing trade trajectory, against the typical duration of within ten days or so.

This is well balanced by ARM's speculative break out from 2027 onwards, once the stock grows into its baked-in premium valuations/ stock prices then and charts new heights beyond the current $180s peaks.

Otherwise, we prefer to downgrade the stock to a Hold here, since its sideways trading cadence may disappoint investors seeking quick returns, worsened by the likely to be expensive and prolonged growth initiatives.

Patience in the mean time.

data.title: Vistra Corp: A Key Energy Player In AI Value Chain
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I am giving Vistra Corp (NYSE: NYSE:VST) a buy rating for the following reasons; AI secular tailwinds, strong company fundamentals and numerous growth opportunities. In an earlier article titled " Hewlett Packard Enterprise: An Upcoming Leader In The AI Server Market " I elaborated details of the AI value chain. I said that the value chain is divided into two sections; Inside the rack and Outside the rack. In the article, I gave more details of inside the rack value chain but didn't dive deep into outside the rack ecosystem. So, for context, I would like to expand outside the rack value chain just to give an indication of where Vistra Corp sits in the AI value chain.

So, when you look at the Outside the rack value chain, there are three categories; Power and thermal management, Manufacturing equipment and test & measurement companies. First, test and measurement companies, there are companies such as Teradyne Inc (TER) and Advantest Corporation (OTCPK:ATEYY). Then secondly, under manufacturing companies, we have companies such as ASML Holdings (ASML), Lam Research Corporation (LRCX) and Applied Materials. And then lastly, we have the power and thermal management companies. Under these companies, we have companies such as Schneider Electric S.E (OTCPK:SBGSF), Vertiv Holdings Co (VRT), nVent Electric plc (NVT), ABB Ltd (OTCPK:ABBNY), Constellation Energy Corporation (CEG), Siemens Aktiengesellschaft (OTCPK:SIEGY), Emerson Electric (EMR), Carrier Global Corporation (CARR), Trane Technologies plc (TT) and Vistra Corp (VST). This is also where I would place Wildan Group (WLDN) which I covered recently. You can read the article here.

Now that I have demonstrated the AI value chain and where VST fits in the ecosystem, I will move on to discuss some of the reasons that I believe warrant VST a Buy rating. First and foremost, is the surging digital power demand. In the article on Wildan Group titled " Wildan Group: Positioned To Capitalize On AI-Driven Energy Gap " I said that data centers consume enormous amount of electricity and energy. And as a result, this drives a surging demand for power infrastructure and efficiency solutions.

"...According to the International Energy Agency report titled "Energy and AI", AI is driving unprecedented electricity demand. And data center electricity use is estimated to triple in the next couple of years. And AI workloads will make up a larger share of total consumption. AI models are becoming even more powerful by the day and as they become more powerful, it means that they demand more energy capacity to keep running these GPUs. The figure below shows the GPU computation cost between 2006-2024 and another training size from 2014-2024..."

Also, the US government has highlighted the urgency of expanding power capacity to support AI development. For instance some of the big hyperscalers such as Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), Google (GOOG) and Meta (META) are heavily investing into data centers. With some of them having their capital budgets up 50% for AI and cloud projects. And the beauty of these trends is that Vistra directly benefits as some of these data centers and new semiconductor fabs come into light.

Secondly, is the strong partnership opportunities. As I have mentioned, data center operators and tech companies require reliable and consistent power supply. And Vistra's fleet of dispatchable generation (including nuclear and modern gas plants ) make the company ideal for some of these tech companies.

"...Finally, we were excited to achieve the successful relicensing of our Perry Nuclear Power Plant during the quarter..."

"...We believe nuclear power's unique combination of carbon-free dispatchable power will continue to play a critical role in meeting our country's electricity needs for decades to come..."

James Burke, CEO, 2Q FY2025 Earnings Call

In fact, according to a report done by McKinsey & Company, hyperscalers are in constant search for dedicated power contracts. And this is one of the reasons why Power Purchase Agreements [PPAs] are on the rise. This is because they now have a very crucial role to play in boosting renewables uptake as shown in the Figure below. The Figure shows the PPA which are a catalyst for renewables development particularly in Spain.

"...We continue to evaluate the remainder of our development portfolio for additional opportunities as long-term power agreements materialize..."

James Burke, CEO, 2Q FY2025 Earnings Call

"...As always, this remains subject to change with any new offtake agreements. As Jim outlined earlier, we see an exciting and expanding opportunity set of growth projects across all generation types within our fleet..."

Kristopher Moldovan, CFO, 2Q FY2025 Earnings Call

And thirdly, there is the integrated Model capturing upside. Unlike many regulated utilities, Vistra is also an independent power producer [IPP]. And I believe its integrated generation plus the retail model provides both scale and stability. This means that VST can generate power at a low cost and then is able to sell directly to end consumers. And this move locks in margins even as prices fluctuates.

"...we believe Vistra's diverse fleet of generation assets, innovative retail business and development capabilities strategically position Vistra for success in the power sector through a variety of opportunities. Customers are looking for multiple tailored solutions to meet their energy needs and whether it is data center contract opportunities with existing assets or higher utilization of our more than 40,000 megawatts of existing assets, we have multiple paths to create value..."

James Burke, CEO, 2Q FY2025 Earnings Call

Now that I have talked about some of the reasons which I believe are crucial in warranting a Buy for VST, let me take you through some of the growth catalysts that I foresee.

Growth Catalysts

First and foremost, the closing of the Lotus gas plant acquisition by Late 2025 will immediately boost the company's capacity and earnings. The Lotus project is expected to add an additional 2.6 GW in key regions as shown below.

"...we continue to see the possibility for our 2026 adjusted EBITDA midpoint opportunity to be $7 billion. It's important to note that this improved opportunity for 2026 does not include any benefit from the pending acquisition of gas-fired power plants from Lotus Infrastructure Partners..."

James Burke, CEO, 2Q FY2025 Earnings Call

"...But as we've done the Energy Harbor deal and the Lotus transaction and some opportunities have arisen, we see our business risk profile and leverage metrics, both improving..."

Kristopher Moldovan, CFO, 2Q FY2025 Earnings Call

As I said, the Lotus project is expected to have a positive impact on earnings. Some of the projected numbers are as below.

I will also be on the lookout for data center power deals. And such contracts would affirm Vistra's growth story. Also as earlier highlighted, management is expected to close on more long term PPAs.

Valuation

When I look at Vistra's growth, let me begin by saying that the company scores an excellent A+ Growth grade. In terms of FWD Revenue Growth, VST stands at 15.91% while the sector median stands at 3.99%. And in terms of y/y Revenue Growth, the company stands at 31.60% while the sector median stands at 5.36%. Also, I note that the company has a 3-5Yr CAGR FWD EPS long-term growth of 35% while the sector median is at 7.18%. And as a result, this clearly shows you the kind of growth prospects that the company has for the future.

In terms of Valuation, I would like to put more emphasis on the PEG ratio since it combines both earnings and growth. In terms of the non-GAAP FWD PEG basis, VST has the ratio at 0.89x while the sector median stands at 2.69x. And this implies a 66.95% discount. On the other hand, the GAAP TTM PEG stands at 0.08x while the sector median stands at 1.30x which implies a 93.54% reduction. In the below Figure, while I note that most of the metrics are in the red zone, I believe the market has only priced a portion of Vistra's growth profile and future plans which explains the healthy PEG ratios.

In terms of Peer comparison, I decided to use of of the other companies in the AI value chain, to get a clearer picture of VST compares to its peers in the power and thermal management segment. And certainly, from the Figure below, Vistra's Forward PEG is lower than most of its peers.

Vistra's Key Risk

The biggest risk I see for Vistra at the moment is tariffs, policy and regulatory risks. As a participant in a competitive market, VST's business activities are tied to regulatory frameworks that can change anytime. As an example in Texas, policymakers are in the process of debating reforms to ensure grid reliability despite surging industrial demand. And a new Texas Law requires large new loads to secure on-site backup generation.

Final Thought

As I said earlier, Vistra is well positioned to capitalize on the AI tailwinds that affect energy and power demand. Also, AI and data centers are extensively energy intensive and require a lot of power. And as a result, this is expected to largely benefit VST. Also, the company's scale, fuel diversity and integrated model positions VST to supply the massive electricity needs of the digital economy. And its fore these reasons that I rate Vistra Corp a Buy.

data.title: LyondellBasell: Best Time To Buy Its 10% Yield Since The COVID Panic Sale
data.description:
LYB stock: dividend yield exceeds 10%

Specialty chemical manufacturers have been facing headwinds lately due to tariff and trade disputes. LyondellBasell Industries N.V. (NYSE:LYB), a leading stock in this space, is no exception. As seen, since these disputes started in April (the so-called Liberation Day), the total return of LyondellBasell has significantly diverged and underperformed the S&P 500. While the S&P 500 has not only recovered from the pullbacks and enjoyed a positive total return of 6.4%, LYB experienced a substantial loss of -27.5%.

In the meantime, the stock has been maintaining and growing its dividend payouts. Its latest dividend payout, declared with an ex-dividend date of August 25, 2025, is $1.37 per share on a quarterly basis. This translates into an annual growth rate of 2.2%. The combination of price corrections and steady payouts has pushed its yield to exceed 10%. As you can see from the following chart below, this is the highest level in at least 10 years except during the COVID pandemic, indicating unusually attractive valuation.

In the rest of this article, I will go a step further to examine LYB’s capital allocation flexibility and total shareholder yield (“TSY”). I will show why its TSY is more than 15%. Thus, I am not only seeing mouthwatering current yield, but also attractive total return potential from the stock.

LYB stock: share buybacks

Most dividend investors focus on, of course , dividend yield. But bear in mind that cash payout is only one of the three main avenues that corporations return capital to shareholders. In the case of LYB, the other two (share repurchase and debt reduction) are also significant compared to its cash dividends.

Let’s start with share buybacks. To provide a broader context, the following insights were taken from a Morgan Stanley study. The study is based on a large sample of the share buyback activities by stocks in the Russell 3000 index. The comment highlighted (the highlighted was added by me” is of relevance in this case. As argued earlier, I believe LYB’s current valuation is unusually attractive, thus I expect the buybacks to result in a wealth transfer from the sellers to existing shareholders in the long term.

The companies in the smallest three deciles realized an average dilution of six percent, while the largest three deciles collectively retired equity. Share buybacks are a way to return capital to shareholders that are equivalent to dividends under strict assumptions.20 But unlike dividends, which treat all shareholders equally, buybacks that occur at any price other than fair value result in a wealth transfer. A company that buys back undervalued stock transfers wealth from the selling shareholders to the ongoing holders. And companies that repurchase overvalued stock transfer wealth from the ongoing shareholders to the selling shareholders.

Under this broader context, let’s look at the specific numbers. As you can tell from the next chart below, LYB has been a consistent buyer of its own shares in the past. Net common buyback yield reached almost 15% in 2020, a result of a fortunate combination of a large share buyback program (of about $3.7 billion as seen in the second chart below) and also the compressed valuation due to the COVID pandemic that year. The buyback has more or less normalized after that, with a current common buyback yield of around 1.87% as seen. LYB has been spending an average of around $300 million on its repurchase program since 2021 as you can see from its cash flow statement in the second chart below.

LYB stock: debt paydowns

Let’s move on to the third component of TSY: debt reductions. From the cash flow statement above, you can see that LYB has been generating sufficient cash to not only pay dividends and buyback shares, but also reducing its debt in recent years. In the past 5 years between 2020 and 2024, LYB has spent a total of about $9 billion in total toward debt repayment. As a result of these substantial repayments, the company’s total liabilities have decreased from about $27.3 billion in 2020 to the current level of $23.3B as seen, a total reduction of ~$4.0 billion. The average market cap of the business is about $24.2 billion in the past 5 years, thus such a debt reduction translated into a shareholder return of over 16.5% during this period or about 3.3% per year on average.

If you think as a business owner, debt reduction is as valuable as dividends. Even though its effect is less visible than receiving a cash handout, the long-term benefits can be even more potent as debt reduction makes the company more valuable to own.

Other risks and final thoughts

Based on my analysis above, TSY of LYB could exceed 15% under current conditions, consisting of 10%+ dividend yield, 1.9% common buyback yield, and 3.3% debt reductions. With this, let me revisit the comment I highlighted in the Morgan Stanley report about wealth transfer. With such an unusually high TSY, I see large odds for the share repurchases to be a net wealth transfer from the exiting shareholders to the staying ones.

In terms of downside risks, there are some near-term headwinds facing the company and the sector. As aforementioned, lately, most multinational specialty chemical manufacturers are feeling the pressure and uncertainties from the Trump administration’s ongoing tariff/trade policies. In addition, LYB has been experiencing some weak demand in Europe and parts of Asia. These headwinds have depressed its financial results in recent quarters and could keep pressuring its EPS in full FY 2025 too. As a reflection, the forecasted EPS of $2.69 for FY 2025 represents -58% year-over-year decrease as seen in the consensus estimate below. As a result of such a large EPS decline, the stock is valued at large premium by historical standards in terms of accounting P/E ratios. For example, the forward P/E is estimated to be 19.82x based on the consensus estimates, translating into a valuation premium of over 94% compared to its 5-year average.

However, I consider its dividend payouts a better measure of its true economic earnings in the long term, and consequently, its dividend yield a better valuation indicator than accounting EPS. Also to echo a point made early on about debt reduction, the use of accounting P/E ratios overlooks the impact of debt paydowns. For instance, the company’s valuation, when measured in EV-based metrics such as EV/EBITDA ratios, reveals a much lower valuation premium as seen in the table below.

To recap, LYB’s 10%+ yield certainly caught our attention. After a closer look, I believe the stock’s appeal goes far beyond its yield. In a nutshell, I see a company with excellent cash flow flexibility to reward shareholders by dividend payouts, buybacks, and also debt reductions. All combined, the TSY could be more than 15%, representing a very skewed risk curve.

data.title: Leonardo: Strong Execution And Guidance Upgrade Support Our Bullish View
data.description:
We are resuming our coverage of Leonardo (OTCPK:FINMF) (OTCPK:FINMY) in light of its Q2 results and the latest developments in the EU defence sector. Market attention remains focused on the trade war and geopolitical issues, including the possibility of a breakthrough toward peace in Ukraine. Despite calls from Europe and Ukraine to be part of the negotiations, Russia and the United States are set to meet in Alaska. On the prospect of peace, European defense stocks have come under pressure.

Despite that, Leonardo almost reached our target price. As a reminder, the case for our buy rating rested on 1) a Supportive Defense Growth Outlook, 2) solid execution and the highest order backlog, and 3) a sum-of-the-parts valuation.

Fig 1

H1 Results and Our Positive Take

In H1, Leonardo delivered solid results with higher top-line sales and orders. The company generated €8.9 billion in revenues (Fig. 2), a 12.9% increase compared to H1 2024 results. Sales were positively impacted by the positive contribution of the Defense Electronics & Security segment. Regarding the backlog, H1 orders increased by 9.7% to €11.2 billion. On a like-for-like basis, Leonardo improved its EBITA by 15% to €581 million. By segment, the company reported a positive output in the Helicopters division, which totally offset the Aeronautics drop. Going down to the P&L report, the company's profit reached €542 million, including a €283 million gain from the sale of Wass. Last year, the company also recorded a €366 million capital gain from the Telespazio consolidation. If we exclude these positive one-offs, Leonardo's net profit increased by 44.4% to €273 million. On the balance sheet, net debt declined and reached €2.2 billion.

Source: Leonardo Q2 results ppt - Fig 2

Why are we still positive?

  1. Before delving into the numbers, it is worth highlighting the key events of the period. While peace prospects could, in theory, weigh on defense sector valuations, we do not see this as the likely outcome. NATO (and the USA) continues to push for higher defense spending, and military capabilities remain essential in the challenging geopolitical environment we are unfortunately navigating. Last week, the Italian Prime Minister called a meeting with the heads of the country’s leading defense companies to discuss defense and security. €150 billion in new investment is available to strengthen the EU defense industrial base and boost joint production. Italy can also resort to debt to commit spending in the defense sector without including it in the GDP/Debt ratio. While this may be negative for the State (for Italy and the EU), it will act as a strong catalyst for the defense industry. Joint procurement also implies joint partnerships. One example is the equal-share joint venture between Leonardo, BAE, and Japan Aircraft Industrial Enhancement for the sixth-generation stealth fighter under the Global Combat Air Programme, which is set to replace Japan’s F2 aircraft and Eurofighter Typhoon starting in 2035;
  2. Leonardo has completed a new acquisition to strengthen its position in cybersecurity. The company fully acquired Axiomatics AB. This marks the third M&A transaction in the cybersecurity space in just a few months, underscoring the sector’s role as one of the pillars of Leonardo’s strategic plan and highlighting the attractive opportunities available in Northern Europe. Leonardo is thus adding a third engine for profit generation, a trend already evident in its Q2 results;
  3. Regarding the results, Leonardo beat analyst estimates on revenue and EBITA by 5% and 12%, respectively. We believe this was due to higher-than-expected GCAP development. The helicopters segment EBITA was also strong. For this reason, order guidance was increased to €22.25 - 22.75 billion with a FOCF of €920-980 million compared to the consensus expectation of €903 million (Fig. 3). Net debt guidance was also reduced. Leonardo's order backlog reached €45 billion, with a book-to-bill ratio 1.3. The upgraded guidance provides supportive evidence for the outlook.

Fig 3

Earnings Change and Valuation

After the Q1 results, we anticipated an upward revision to guidance, which Q2 has confirmed (Fig. 3). We have also made a few adjustments to our projected figures accordingly. Our estimates do not include positive one-off gains (recorded in H1 2025). That said, starting with Leonardo key holdings, we highlight equity stakes in DRS (71.32%), Hensoldt AG (22.8%), and Avio (28.76%) for a total consideration of €10.05 billion. This is €2.25 billion higher than the Q1 results update. Given the supportive backlog and higher guidance, we might increase our 2026 EBITA projection. Despite the high visibility, we leave our estimates unchanged at €2.1 billion.

Rolling forward our valuation with an unchanged EV/EBITA of 10x, we derive a standalone value of €21 billion. With a better FCF generation (confirmed by lower H1 seasonality) and no additional acquisitions, net debt is expected to fall to €1.1 billion by year-end. This is a lower basis than our previous expenditure of €1.5 billion. Leonardo's total enterprise value, including equity holdings, is estimated at €31.05 billion. The company's equity value reached €29.95 billion with a lower net debt. This implied a 9% upside and a target price of $30.4 for the FINMY ADR (€52).

Risks

Downside risks include: 1) peace prospects and possible cuts to defense spending; 2) lower performance from the Aerostructures segment, 3) integration risks from recent M&A in cybersecurity, 4) FX and accounting change, 5) COGS price change and wage inflation, 6) non-ideal corporate governance (given Italy equity stake), 7) supply-chain shock, and 8) valuation is subject to equity holdings stock price performance and the company derives 25% of earnings from associate investments and JV.

Conclusion

Leonardo reported solid numbers, and the guidance upgrade is a key positive. While sentiment remains high, we remain bullish in the near and long term.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
